text: Chapter 10 teaches self-accompaniment techniques for solo guitar performance, covering bass-line construction and voicing strategies to accompany melody. The foundational approaches include root-movement bass (simplest form), doubled bass notes (swing-era style), root/fifth alternation (common in folk and country), and walking bass with chromatic approach notes (characteristic of jazz). The chapter introduces anticipation and delay devices to vary rhythmic feel, arpeggiation for slower tunes with melody emphasis, and sparse two-note inner-voice voicing that produces full arrangements despite minimalist voice count. Advanced techniques include moving tenths (adapted from swing-era piano practice of Fats Waller and Art Tatum), and rootless close voicing using fourths and tritones—a device that works especially well in ensemble settings where a bassist provides harmonic foundation. Throughout, the chapter emphasizes that effect and musical coherence matter more than rigid adherence to strict interval patterns, and that voice-leading collisions between melody and accompaniment can be reframed as valid arrangement solutions rather than problems.
